column2: Back

- Gëschter huet de Pol am Gaart geschafft.
- Onglécklecherweis ass hien an en Harespel snotst geroden,
- an hien ass esou fiel gepikt ginn,
- datt hien huet missen an Spidol goen.
- Glisa ass de Moie mam Auto an d'Staat gefuer.
- Hat ass vun engem Hond op der Stroos ofgelenkt ginn
- a konnt net mat Zeit bremsen.
- E Putter stung am Wee,
- an hat ass agerannt.
- Weid e Jacques de Moien a seng Stroos ofbeye wolt,
- huet jende Vëlosfuerer op der Vëlospiss iwer sinn
- an him virfährt gehol.
- Dobäi ass de Vëlosfuerer ugestoß ginn,
- hien ass op de Burdem gefall
- a war, wuel jenne Kasun hat, e puer Minute lang fusisch.
- Julie huet e wichdesche Message um Handy kritt.
- Hat huet natierlisch op den Handy geguckt
- an ass dobäi mam Kapp an d'Zier gerannt.
- Hat huet richtig Stäre gesin
- an hat e puer Sekonne lang den Tommel.
- De Ross huet engem Kollege gehollef, e Bild opzehänken.
- Hien huet Bild ugehalen
- an de Kollege huet him mam Hummer op de Fanger geschloen.
- De Fanger huet geblut
- an et huet immens weh gedoen.
- Si sinn allen zwei schwach gefall.
- Yesterday the police officer worked in the garden.
- Unfortunately he got caught in a harebrained tumble and he was so badly painted that he had to go to the hospital.
- Glisa drove the morning with the car into the state.
- Hat was distracted by a dog on the street
- and could not brake in time.
- A pothole stood in the way,
- and he crashed.
- When a Jacques of the morning in his street wanted to sweep,
- he hit a cyclist on the bicycle path
- and forced him ahead.
- Meanwhile the cyclist was pushed,
- he fell on the pavement
- and, well, at least it must have hurt for a few minutes.
- Julie received a disturbing message on her mobile.
- She naturally looked at the mobile
- and ran with her head into the edge.
- She really saw stars
- and for a few seconds touched her thumb.
- The boss helped a colleague hang a picture.
- He held up the picture
- and the colleague hit him with the lobster on the finger.
- The finger bled
- and it hurt a lot.
- They all two fell weakly.

- Guten Moien, hei ass den Renotzwielef.
- Moien, ech muss ganz séier en Dokter hunn. Mäi Mann leit hannert mir, hie gett séier krank.
- Bleift lo mol ganz rouisch, Madame. Ech brauch fir d'éischt e puer Informatiounen. Wou sidd Dir? Ween ass d'Adress?
- Ech sinn doheem bei mir, zu Diddeleng op der Hauptstroß op Nummer 11.
- Wësst Dir, wat geschitt ass?
- Mä jo, ech giesset net genee. Mir heileie Schiermerle woen, an da hannert sech des Droger.
- Also e Mann bei Bewosstsinn, schwätzt hie mat Iech?
- Nee, hie ass bewosstlos. Oh mei, hie gett och belegt. Ech muss en entfensterachen soll.
- Berouischt Iech, Madame. Ech schécke nescht direkt e Samu an eng Ambulanz. Si sinn an ungefeier 15 Minute do. Kënnt Dir mir nach Ären Numm soen, wann ech gelift?
- Mein Numm ass Kap Gloeden.
- Gut, Madame Gloeden, ech bleiwe nach e bësse mat iesch um Telefon.
- Good morning, this is Renotzwielef.
- Hello, I need a doctor very quickly. My husband is behind me, he is getting very sick.
- Please stay calm for a moment, madam. I need some information first. Where are you? What is the address?
- I am at home with me, in Dudelange on the main street at number 11.
- Do you know what happened?
- Yes, I did not drink very well. We help Schiermerle where, and then behind six of the drugstore.
- So a man with consciousness, is he talking to you?
- No, he is unconscious. Oh my, he is also injured. I must inform someone.
- Please take care, madam. I will send a Samu and an ambulance right away. They are about 15 minutes away. Can you tell me your name, please?
- My name is Kap Gloeden.
- Okay, Madame Gloeden, I will stay a little longer with you on the phone.

- Lauschtert zoien ennso.
- Et gine poe enner Schäder tëscht dem Zeitungsartikel an der Zoien ennso.
- Noter joze, e vergleicht modernen Nopa.
- Häbi lo, die seid de gute Kolleg am Maddärbeste vum Herr Mais, den du mütesch von der Stiege fahres.
- Könnte de Res van der Stiefskwiz erklären, wie die jetzituazion alliert tut?
- Major séch ja, also mir wäre grat dobei, zu summen en grodt Stun nun zu freischen, die mir dünn und der Mauer behältsche solten.
- Hin hat die grodt Wohlnam am Grapp, a weh hinne schrot no Hanne gemähchut, as hin iwa de Faf ehmag gefall.
- Et wäre nömme knapp zwei Meter, also gwöne zu heich.
- Die Pjergut zu Penge gejaut, an ich sie natürlich direkt e Roofkucke gang.
- Hin läuft durch en zwe Hekken, a konnt gwönet mei opstohen.
- Ich jo meng Jacket ob je geloest, an hun direkt en Ambulanz geruf.
- Hin hatte poe Schrumen am Gesicht, en grots Wonnus enger Schülle, an hin hot gesot, dat him dat hätzt Peh nie mensch weh dohe ging.
- Zeng Minute mir späht hätt Ambulanz oschon ob der Platz, sie hunn je kurz analyscht, an dun direkt matgehol.

- He had the big living room on the grip, and when he knocked at Hanne, he did it, as if the Faf had fallen into place.
- It would be only about two meters high, so just a bit tall.
- The Pjergut to Penge was called, and I of course went up directly to the Roof to look.
- He walks through two hedges, and could easily stand my place up.
- I took off my jacket and called an ambulance directly.
- He had a few scrapes on his face, a large wound under a sleeve, and he said it hurt him that no one helped him there.
- Ten minutes later, an ambulance was already on the square; they analyzed briefly and then helped right away.

- Dat war dat Eppes.
- De Pierre ass jo haut vun der Stieg gefall.
- Egelik wär et net ze vill schlimm.
- Mee do no ass Police komm an ech hu missen op all hir Froen äntweren.
- Wat hun se dech dann alles gefroot?
- Mä si wollten wüssen, wat mir grad gemerkt hun, well wat geschitt ass.
- Si hun och gefroot, ob jen klaischt Gewischt verluer huet oder iwat eppes gefall ass.
- Konst du ihnen dat da soen?
- Mä jo, se es ja. Ech hun et jo gesin.
- An do hun sie och nach gefroot, fir wat de Pierre kee Kask uun hätt.
- Op enger Stief vun zwee Meter brauch en da keen Helm.
- Et wies du, du dass jo awer gefäierlech, dat wüsste jo haut gesin.
- Ja, ja.
- An da wollten sie och nach wüssen, ob je fusisch wär, wie je nun Bodemloch.
- An ob jen Alkohol gedronk hätt.
- Also wirklich.
- Ja, drink dir dann net heians do e Beja iwat dem Schaffen?
- Natierlech net.
- A wann den Ambulanz geruff huet, dat wollten sie och nach wüssen.
- An do hun sie misse nach gefroot, wéi lang es schon do schaffen.
- Ech froe mich, wat dat mam Akzident zu din huet.
- That was something.
- Pierre is indeed just fell from the ladder today.
- It could be not too bad.
- But then the police came and I had to answer all their questions.
- What did they ask you then?
- But they wanted to know what we had just noticed, because what happened.
- They also asked whether the small weapon was lost or something fell.
- Can you tell them that then?
- But yes, she is. I saw it.
- And then they also asked why Pierre would not wear a helmet.
- On a two-meter step, you don’t need a helmet.
- You know, you know that it was dangerous, that would be seen today.
- Yes, yes.
- And then they also wanted to know whether you were barefoot, like you are now on the ground.
- And whether you had drunk alcohol.
- Well really.
- Yes, aren’t you drinking a sip for the job here?
- Of course not.
- And when the ambulance was called, they also wanted to know.
- And then they had to ask how long you have been working there.
- I wonder what that accident has to do with yours.

- Wéi heescht Dir?
- Mäi Numm ass Sophie.
- Vu wou kënnt Dir?
- Ech kommen aus Lëtzebuerg.
- Sidd Dir Schwäizer?
- Nee, ech sinn Lëtzebuergerin.
- An Dir?
- Ech sinn däitsch.
- Wéi al sidd Dir?
- Ech sinn 25 Joer al.
- Wéi geet et Iech?
- Et geet mir gutt, merci.
- An Iech?
- Och gutt, merci.
- What is your name?
- My name is Sophie.
- Where are you from?
- I come from Luxembourg.
- Are you Swiss?
- No, I am Luxembourger.
- And you?
- I am German.
- How old are you?
- I am 25 years old.
- How are you?
- I am well, thanks.
- And you?
- Also well, thanks.

- Ech sinn d'Léonie Schmidt.
- Schons 36 Joer a mäin Eezwaigniant vu Beruf.
- Et war schon emmer mäin Dram, fir mat anerem Menschen ze schaffen an hinnen ze hëllefen.
- Ech schaffe elo zanter zwëe Joer an enge grosse Spidol heuernder Staat.
- Fir drun hun ech fënnef Joer an engem Altersheem geschafft.
- Dat war och ganz flott, mee et war leider net esou ofwiesslungsräich wéi an der Klinik.
- Och war mäin Dach elo viel mei stressesch als wéi fir drun.
- Mäin Dach fängt dachst schommojes um fënnef Auer un.
- Am Spidol schaffe mir ob Schichten.
- Fréi Schicht hun ech am léifsten, weil ech dann mittes frei hun an eppes mat der Famille maache kann.
- Mäi Beruf ass immens vielseitig.
- Moies maache mir fir d'éischten Tour ob eisem Stock.
- Ech kontrollére in Vitalwerte von eiser Patientin, dat heescht ech moose in de Blutdrock an de Bolz.
- Bei verschiedene Patiente kontrollére in ech och den Zocker.

- I am Léonie Schmidt.
- Beautiful 36 years and my everyday life of work.
- It has always been my dream to work with other people and help them.
- I have been working for two years now in a large hospital in this state.

- That was also quite nice, but it was not as varied as in the hospital.
- Also, my workload now is much more stressful than before.
- My workload starts already at five o'clock in the afternoon.
- In the hospital we work in shifts.
- Early shift I like best, because then I have free time to do something with the family.
- My profession is extremely versatile.
- In the morning we do the first tour on our ward.
- I check the vital signs of our patient, which means I take the blood pressure and pulse.
- With different patients I also check the glucose.

- Duennowhellefen eschte Patiente beim Opstoen a beim Un- oder Ausdoen.
- Dachs muss ich Patiente waschen oder ich hëllefen ihnen, Zähne zu waschen, sich anzukremen oder sich zu kämmen.
- Mir hëllefen der Leute bei allem, wat se net eleng maachen kënnen.
- Mir mussen och Martinen op Toilette goen oder Tündele wiesselen.
- Dat ass net sou flott, mee dat gehéiert och zur Aarbecht.
- An zwee Stonnen verdeelen ech Dürsen ëmstack.
- Heiansdo muss ech der Patiente och Zousuergen, an ech muss vill méi Leit animéieren, fir genuch ze drénken an se un hir Medikamenter erënneren.
- Als A2ier däerfen mir de Patiente selwer keng Medikamenter ginn, mee mir mussen oppassen, dass se hir Medikamenter, déi se vum Dokter verschriwwen kruten, regelméisseg huelen.
- Am Spidol sinn och vill Leit, déi net méi opstoen kënnen.
- All zwou Stonnen mussen mir d’Leit ëmleien, fir dass se net wunde ginn.
- Dat ass immens ustrengend an uewen drop e Stach an der Eck.
- Wann ech Patiente fir hir Operatiounen preparéieren, da muss ech se raséieren oder wäschen.
- Vir an no enger Operatioun sinn d’Patiente normalerweis immens opgereegt, an ech probéieren dann mat hinnen ze schwätzen an se e bëssen ze berouegen.
- Am Nomëtteg ginn et vill Patiente ronderëm, do mussen mir d’Bett frësch ofzéien an d’Kummer fir den nächsten Patient preparéieren.
- Ech sinn ëmmer frou, wann eng Persoun rëm heem goe kann.
- Dat heescht, dass se rëm gesond ass, an dunn kréie mir och Knipäsche oder eng kleng Kaddo als Merci fir d’Aarbecht.
- During morning rounds I help real patients with getting up and with undressing or dressing.
- Then I have to wash patients or help them wash their teeth, put on makeup, or comb their hair.
- We help people with everything they cannot manage themselves.
- We also have to take patients to the toilet or change diapers.
- That isn’t so nice, but it’s also part of the job.
- Every two hours I turn the patients in bed.
- Sometimes I also assist patients, and I have to encourage many more people to drink enough and to remind them of their medications.
- As an A2 assistant, we may not give patients medication ourselves, but we must ensure that they take the medications prescribed by the doctor regularly.
- In the hospital there are also many people who can’t get up anymore.
- Every two hours we must reposition the people to prevent them from getting sores.
- That is extremely exhausting and, on top of that, a stabbing pain in the corner.
- When I prepare patients for their operations, I have to shave them or wash them.
- Before and after a operation the patients are usually extremely excited, and I try to talk to them and calm them a little.
- In the afternoon there are many patients around, so we have to make the bed fresh and prepare the care items for the next patient.
- I am always happy when someone can go home again.
- That means they are back to health, and then we also get cookies or a small gift as thanks for the work.

- One
- A large part of occupational diseases are skin diseases.
- This can, for example, be eczema with red cracks or cracks that are painful.
- Often people affected are those who work in industry,

- But also constant contact with water or sea irritates the skin.

- Two.
- When you work in a butchery or in a medical laboratory, the risk is quite high to come into contact with splashes.
- Additionally, you can also get infected with a disease that transmits some blood, such as hepatitis or HIV.
- There are therefore very strict rules in place to prevent infection.
- Also medical instruments must be sterile.
- Syringes should be disposed of immediately after use.
- In addition, the staff should protect themselves with protective clothing, gloves, and a respirator.

- For help.
- The back pain sheets are those of the muscles and the skeleton.
- Many occupational groups are affected by this.
- On the one hand, it can be people who have to work physically hard,
- but on the other hand also people who take the same position for a long time.
- Heavy lifting strains the spine.
- Sitting in front of the computer for a long time can lead to back pain or tension in the neck.
- In order for that not to happen, one should change position often.
- Additionally, one can strengthen their musculature through strength training.
- It can also help when one works at the desk in an upright position.
- In any case, it is important to always sit/stand correctly, no matter what one does.

- Dat kënnen engersäits Leit sinn, déi schweier Kierpelesch mussen schaffen,
- awer anerersäits och Leit, déi laang immer déi selwecht Haltung anhuelen.
- Schweier Hewe belast Kierbelsäil.
- Laang virum Computersetze kann zu Réckwee oder Verspannungen am Genéck féieren.
- Fir dat net geschitt, sollt en Positioun dacks vierzelen.
- Ausserdeem kann en duerch Muskeltraining seng Muskulatur stäerken.
- Et kann och hëllefen, wann en um Büro am Stull schafft.
- Et ass op jidde Fall wichteg, sech ëmmer richteg ze halen, egal wat e mëscht.
column2:

- Your most frequent occupational illnesses are those of the muscles and the skeleton.
- Many occupational groups are affected here.
- On one hand, these can be people who have to work with heavy machinery,
- but on the other hand also people who take the same posture for a long time.
- Heavy spinal load.
- Sitting at the computer for long periods can lead to back pain or tension in the neck.
- To prevent this from happening, a position should be changed frequently.
- Additionally, muscle training can strengthen your muscles.
- It can also help if you work at the office in a chair.
- In any case, it is important to always stay upright correctly, no matter what you are doing.

- E groussen Deel vun de Beruffskrankheete si Krankheete vun der Haut.
- Dat kann zum Beispill en Ekzema mat roude Platzen oder Platzen, déi béisse sinn.
- Dacks si Leit betraff, déi an der Industrie schaffen a mat aggressive Chemikalien ze dinn hunn.
- Durch de Kontakt mat dëse Chemikalien kënne allergesch Reaktiounen entstoen.
- Mä och de stännege Kontakt mat Waasser oder Seef reizt d'Haut.
- Dofir ass de Risiko bei Coifferen oder beim Butzpersonal relativ héich.
- Präventiv soll een Händschen undoen an d'Haut gutt fleegen.
- A large part of occupational diseases are skin diseases.
- That can for example be eczema with red blisters or ruptures that hurt.
- Often people affected are those who work in industry and deal with aggressive chemicals.
- Through contact with these chemicals allergic reactions can develop.
- But also the constant contact with water or soap irritates the skin.
- Therefore the risk is relatively high for hairdressers or buttocks staff.
- Preventively one should wash hands and let the skin dry well.

- A wann ass dat mat dir lass?
- Wéi wat kreist du?
- De Poli huet mir d'Hoer gebroch.
- Oh, wat ass da geschitt?
- Du hues dach soss eppes geschitt mat dem Poli.
- Och jo, mee hie wollt mir schons en dozwi deischte Kalsch verlo.
- Als Zabrieche mode Koppwadchen huet.
- Vläicht hätt e mir mol gefrot, ebe Koppwad bréngt.
- Hie huet op vill vun der neier Aarbechtskollegin do geschwat.
- Gemoer e ginn op der Zon, mir schonn ne Kräb net.
- Ah jo, dat. Ech mengen, do triffs du de Nol op de Kapp.
- Hajoens do Sounisch mir, hien ass einfach nach gréng an den Oren.
- Vläicht musst du him mol op de Fang fillen.
- Jo, also ech holen elo kee Bladmeicher an de Mond.
- Mua schwätzen es mat him.
- Mir musse him bestëmmt alles éier en Ust zielt.
- Richteg so, du solls de Bäknet halen.
- Sou eng Geschicht soll ech net op dech zwéngen.
- Jo, sou kanns du bestëmmt eng Erklärung aus der Fanger ronnen.
- Ech gräifen dacks en Holz, wann ech dran denken.
- Mee mua, sou eng him seng Meenung.
- Da kann hie säin Staat sohnat do eppes schreiwen.
- So, elo spitz du mol den Ouer an lauschter mir mol gutt no.
- De Poli ass net déi richteg Chefredaktioun.
- Jo, dat rast.
- Ech huelen besser Pfäffer an de Krop.
- Richteg fälsch.
- Schlo der dir et am Krop, an erkenns du mo en iso Bän.
- Gutt, dat frënnt dem Ianer Dämm gräifen an mir hëllefen.
- Do fiesi schund do.
- Am de Poli?
- Wat jens de Poli?
- And when did that happen to you?
- What do you mean?
- The police broke my hair.
- Oh, what happened then?
- You have had other problems with the police, haven't you?
- Yes, but he already wanted to give me a second cold shoulder.
- Like breaking a headband.
- Maybe he should have asked me first before bringing a headband.
- He talked a lot about the new colleague at work.
- Yesterday he went to the zone, we don't have any trouble yet.
- Ah yes, that. I think you hit the nail on the head there.
- Honestly, he's just green behind the ears.
- Maybe you need to put him in his place.
- Yes, so I won't hold my tongue now.
- Tomorrow I'll talk to him.
- We definitely have to tell him everything before he tells a story.
- That's right, you should keep your mouth shut.
- I shouldn't force such a story on you.
- Yes, that way you can definitely come up with an explanation.
- I often get annoyed when I think about it.
- But now, that's just his opinion.
- Then he can write something about his state.
- So, now prick up your ears and listen carefully to me.
- The police are not the right editorial board.
- Yes, that's true.
- I'd better take pepper in my throat.
- Right or wrong.
- Keep it in your throat, and you'll recognize it tomorrow.
- Good, that helps Ianer Dämm to grab and help us.
- That's nasty stuff there.
- At the police?
- What do you mean, the police?

- I arrive in London at a fire that killed at least four people.

- The fire broke out at night in a high-rise building.
- Why is it not known yet.

column1:
- Zu London komme bei engem Feier op d'mannst véier Leit ëm d'Liewen.
- Iwwer drësseg Persoune goufe schwéier verletzt.
- D'Feier ass nuets an engem Héichhaus ausgebrach.
- Firwat ass nach net gewosst.

- I arrive in London at a fire that killed at least four people.
- Over thirty people were seriously injured.
- The fire broke out at night in a high-rise building.
- Why is it not known yet.

- Den Hervé Klein schafft an der Zentral vum 112.
- An dat mëndewiu schwätzt Hien iwwer de Cgtist aus dem Funderettungsbänkster.
- Herr Klein, am Joer 2013 ass jo de ganze System vun der Rettungsbänkster Hëtz-Lëtzebursch reforméiert ginn.
- Kënnt dir eis die gräusste Verännerungen beschreiven a soen, wie dir de Changement erfrönt?
- Jo, natierlisch, dat war e ganz gräßere Form, mä es probieren, mesch kuiz zu fassen.
- Also, engerseits sind 2013 all Rettungsbänkster für Lützebursch enat dem Cgtist zu Sommer geschlossen ginn.
- Dat heischt Protektion zivil, freiwillig Pompjën, staateberufspompjën, Fluchhefe Pompjën an de Sarmü.
- Annererseits geht et von enn 2020 u, mam CNS zu Kasbrüch, endliche grösse sit, wo die wichtigsten Elemente vom Rettungswese regroupiert sinn.
- Do sinne lo z.B. Berufspompjën, Zentral 112, an Nochtadministration an Formationszentren.
- Freier wären all die Strukturen ob verschiedene Platzen am Land.
- Dat war schwer, für die Kommunikation in den Nähn.
- Elo, wo alle sine dengem daas, as dat mei einfach, an den administrativen Opwand as och nit mei so heisch wie hierdruen.
- Kënnt dir vannscht gut bissen drüber goen, wat die wichtigsten Missionen a Responsabilitäten vom Cgtist sinn?
- Et as schwer zu soen, wat die wichtigste Missionen sinn, weil et immer drum geht, für Leben zu retten.
- An dem Sinn sinn se natierlich all wichtig.
- Et kinkt soen, dat Mission vom Cgtist as, Menschen an eng Nothol zu Lötzebursch zu hellefen.
- E Feier löschen, bei enn Maxident hellefen, Leitmedizinisch versüschen, an an Spidol bringen, an esu weider.
- Mir hellefen aber nit nemmen zu Lötzebursch, man mir hellefen zum Beispiell och bei Katastrophen am Ausland.
- Prävention a Formatioun sie nach wichtig.
- Et as immer besser, wann et gunne zu engem schroen Nothol kennt.
- Dofir müssen Leute wissen, we sie sich schützen oder ob man es Dättschlens verhindern können.
- An Fralodanawa musste seriöe Notfall anstritt, kennt schiderend Telefonnummer 112.
- Dat kling kann och scho von kleinem Münd an der Schule gesucht, an a ganz Europa gilt dieselbe Telefonnummer.
- Gött so viel benutzt?
- Ojo, an dem Mojeng kreim ja 1400 Enersächstes Telefonn pro Daare ran.
- Eng Equip von opmansch Feialeit hält permanenten Notri den Geint.
- All Uroof geht orgistisch dreiert, an da gött den Alarm ausgeleist, wann et muss sinn, an die richte Rettungsdenkstäginer rausgeschickt.
- Pro Dhaach ginn es auch knapp 200 Interventionen gemaach.
- Et handelt sich jo nid immer um Notfall, wann Leute op den 112 Uruffen.
- Et kinna och dei Just wisse wollen, wat für Doktoren, Apdikten oder Spidela dengschtun oder we jesu für en esthelfkur aschreive kann.
- Dofir as den Notruf 112 awer nid geduischt.
- Die seriödest organisierte gratis Esthelfkur an op Lötzebursch Deitsch, Fransäisch an Englisch Iwat ganz Johr an am ganse Land.
- Für wat gödd es so viel wert op die Kure geluscht?
- Alldach geschiersch Löm Aksidenta, an so enger Situation as et wichtig, für seje errischisch zu handelen.
- Jidder Reisdoll dat können.
- Dofir bide mir viel Kuren op verschiedene Platzen nun, zu verschiedenen Zeiten an auf verschiedenen Sprachen.
- An desse Kure leja den, we jesu bei en Aksident richtig verhailt, a we jesu verlässt richtig versuuscht, wiese Rettungsdenkstor es.
- Kuren dauere 16 Stunden an zum Schluss as en Test.
- Wann en depakt, da kied en en Diplom, de funnef Johr gültig as.
- Mr. Hervé Klein works at the center of the 112.
- And this is where he talks about the Cgtist from the Funderettungsbänkster.
- Mr. Klein, in 2013 the entire system of the Rettungsbänkster Hëtz-Lëtzebursch was reformatted.
- Can you describe to us the biggest changes and say what prompted the change?
- Yes, of course, it was a very large form, but I’ll try to grasp it.
- So, on the one hand, in 2013 all Rettungsbänkster for Luxembourg were closed at the Cgtist in Sommer.
- This means civil protection, voluntary pompions, state vocational pompions, flight rescue pompions, and the Sarmü.
- On the other hand, from 2020 onward, with the CNS to Kasbrüch, a final size is created where the most important elements of the rescue system are regrouped.
- There you have, for example, Vocational pompjons, Central 112, and Night Administration and Formation Centers.
- All those structures are spread across different places in the country.
- It was difficult for communication in the neighborhoods.
- Now, where all that is, it’s easier, and the administrative overhead isn’t as heavy as before.
- Can you perhaps go a little into what the main missions and responsibilities of the Cgtist are?
- It’s hard to say what the main missions are, because it’s always about saving lives.
- And in that sense they are all important.
- It can be said that the mission of the Cgtist is to help people in urgent need in Luxembourg.
- A fire extinguished, to help in a major accident, medically assist, and to bring to the hospital, and so on.
- We help not only Luxembourg, but also with disasters abroad.
- Prevention and training are also important.
- It’s always better when it can lead to a good emergency response.
- For that, people need to know how to protect themselves or how to prevent it.
- In France and Luxembourg there must be serious emergency lines, the number 112.
- That line can also be searched from a young age at school, and across Europe the same number applies.
- Do you use it that much?
- Oh yes, in that moment you realize you have about 1400 emergencies per day.
- A team of outstanding volunteers maintains permanent emergency readiness.
- All calls are organized threefold, and the alarm is released when needed, and the right rescue team is sent out.
- On average there are about 200 interventions made.
- It’s not always about emergencies when people call 112.
- It could also be about whether you want to know which doctors, pharmacies, or hospitals you should contact for first aid instructions.
- Therefore the 112 emergency call is not ignored.
- The most serious organized free first aid in Luxembourg in German, French, and English for almost all year across the country.
- Why is it so valuable to invest in these cures?
- Every day there are accidents, and in such a situation it’s important to act correctly.
- Anyone can do that.
- Therefore we do many courses in different places at different times and in different languages.
- In these courses they learn how to behave properly in an accident, and how to provide proper first aid until professionals arrive, which are rescue structures.
- Courses last 16 hours and finally serve as a test.
- When you complete it, you receive a diploma valid for five years.
